title: Feature Table Deep Dive
subtitle: How cleaned telemetry becomes forecasting-ready rows

nodes.label: Feature Table
nodes.definition: The final structured row set used as input to practical forecasting workflows.
nodes.whyItMatters: A consistent feature table enables repeatable forecasting and risk classification.
nodes.safeSentence: Feature tables package cleaned telemetry into decision-support inputs.
nodes.commonTrap: Treating the feature table as static even when telemetry definitions or bucket rules changed.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I describe the feature table as the contract between telemetry prep and forecasting. It preserves key, time bucket, feature signals, and row quality so downstream risk calls are explainable.
relatedQuestions: How do you prevent feature drift after telemetry schema changes?
Which fields are required before a row can be used for forecasting?

note.summary: Use one row per application and time bucket with explicit quality status.

image.caption: Example feature table shape for forecasting-ready telemetry.

nodes.label: Application Key
nodes.definition: A stable identifier that ties each feature row to a specific application.
nodes.whyItMatters: Application keys preserve ownership context and aggregation integrity.
nodes.safeSentence: Application keys keep forecasting rows aligned to accountable services.

nodes.label: Time Bucket
nodes.definition: A normalized interval field (for example hourly) used to align telemetry points.
nodes.whyItMatters: Buckets ensure signals are comparable and joinable across features.
nodes.safeSentence: Time buckets create consistent slices for trend and peak calculations.
nodes.commonTrap: Mixing 5-minute and hourly buckets in one training set without normalization.
relatedQuestions: How do you choose bucket width for capacity forecasting?
How do you handle sparse telemetry in fixed buckets?

note.summary: Choose a bucket cadence that matches decision frequency and telemetry stability.

nodes.label: Rolling Average
nodes.definition: A smoothed trend metric over a moving window for each application and bucket.
nodes.whyItMatters: Rolling averages reduce noise and reveal directional behavior.
nodes.safeSentence: Rolling averages provide stable trend context in each row.

nodes.label: Rolling Peak
nodes.definition: A recent-window maximum feature that captures near-worst-case pressure.
nodes.whyItMatters: Peak-sensitive features help identify practical capacity risk.
nodes.safeSentence: Rolling peaks preserve stress signals that averages can hide.

nodes.label: P95
nodes.definition: A high-percentile usage signal included for robust upper-range behavior tracking.
nodes.whyItMatters: P95 balances outlier tolerance with meaningful high-load visibility.
nodes.safeSentence: P95 gives a practical upper-usage indicator per feature row.

nodes.label: Quality Flag
nodes.definition: A row-level status marker indicating whether required fields and checks passed.
nodes.whyItMatters: Quality flags prevent low-trust rows from silently influencing forecasts.
nodes.safeSentence: Quality flags make row reliability explicit before forecasting use.
nodes.commonTrap: Letting failed-quality rows pass because the percentage looks small.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I use quality flags to gate forecasting inputs. Failed rows are either repaired or excluded with traceable rationale.
relatedQuestions: What minimum quality checks should run before scoring?
How do you report rejected rows to stakeholders?

paths.label: Build Feature Table
paths.description: Turn identified, bucketed telemetry into validated feature rows.
paths.rehearsalPrompt: Explain how a feature table row is built from telemetry identity, time shape, and quality checks.
